ask_next.question: Is there difficulty breathing, throat tightening, or wheezing?
ask_next.purpose: Confirm systemic anaphylaxis vs. isolated localized reaction
ask_next.why: Respiratory or cardiovascular involvement confirms anaphylaxis — epinephrine is required

response_guides.note: Respiratory compromise confirmed — EpiPen immediately if available, call 911
Isolated hives without respiratory symptoms — monitor closely for escalation, still seek care

ask_next.question: Do they have an EpiPen or epinephrine auto-injector available?
ask_next.why: EpiPen is the only effective first-line treatment for anaphylaxis

response_guides.note: Use EpiPen NOW — inject outer thigh, hold 10 seconds, then call 911
No EpiPen available — call 911 immediately, anaphylaxis cannot be managed without epinephrine

ask_next.question: What were they exposed to — food, insect sting, or a medication?
ask_next.purpose: Identify trigger for EMS notification and remove if possible
ask_next.why: EMS needs the allergen for treatment planning; some triggers need immediate removal

response_guides.note: Medication-induced anaphylaxis — often more severe and rapid, maximum urgency
Insect sting — remove stinger by scraping if visible, do not squeeze. EpiPen if available.
